extracted_rules.resource: Right to indict on own behalf
verbatim: For, I take it, if I had wished to bring a malicious suit, I should not have listed slaves worth two minae and a half, the sum at which the claimant himself has fixed their value, and myself have run the risk of losing a thousand drachmae, and forfeiting the right ever again to indict anyone on my own behalf.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author refers to a standard legal penalty in the Athenian court system where a prosecutor who fails to obtain a minimum of one-fifth of the votes (implying a malicious or baseless suit) faces a fine and the loss of future right to bring similar indictments.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to share of confiscated property
verbatim: And now that I have done so, if I can prove that the slaves belong to Arethusius, to whom they are stated in the information to belong, I relinquish to the state the three fourths which under the law are given to the private citizen filing the information; for myself it is enough to have taken vengeance.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly mentions a law that rewards a private citizen who successfully provides information (phasis) leading to the confiscation of property with three-fourths of the value of that property.

verbatim: You know, he said, that the laws enact that a person ransomed from the enemy shall be the property of the ransomer, if he fail to pay the redemption money.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text cites a specific law regarding citizens or individuals captured in war: if they are ransomed and fail to repay the ransomer, they become the legal property (a form of debt-bondage or slave-like status) of the person who paid for their release.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from torture during legal proceedings
verbatim: I told them that, if my suit against them had been a private one, I should have accepted the slaves for the torture, if they had offered them, but that, as it was, both the slaves and the information belonged to the state; and therefore the examination by the torture should be conducted by a public official.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author discusses the standard procedure of 'basanos', where slaves are subjected to physical torture to provide evidence in Greek legal cases, specifically noting that for state-owned slaves, the process must be conducted by public officials.

verbatim: And yet the laws enact that any man’s estate shall be confiscated who, after guaranteeing any sum due to the state, does not make good his guarantee; so that, even if the slaves belonged to them, they ought to be state-property, if the laws are of any use.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a legal rule where a citizen who acts as a guarantor for a debt to the treasury (the state) faces the total confiscation of their own estate if the debt is not repaid.
